evals.prompt: Write a formal academic research report on 'energy efficiency of large language model inference' from these three sources: (1) Dao (2023), FlashAttention-2, reports reduced GPU memory bandwidth consumption on A100 hardware, https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08691; (2) Jiang et al. (2024), a mixture-of-experts model, reports that routing activates only a fraction of total parameters per token, https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.04088; (3) a 2026 study arguing transformer inference energy scales roughly with sequence length. Use the default author-date (APA) citation style.
evals.expected_output: A complete academic report with all six IMRaD sections in order (Abstract, Background/Related Context, Method, Findings, Discussion, References), author-date in-text citations such as '(Dao, 2023)' resolving to a full References list, and every claim in Findings traceable to one of the three supplied sources.
expectations: Contains all six required sections in order: Abstract, Background/Related Context, Method, Findings, Discussion, References
Uses author-date in-text citations (e.g. '(Dao, 2023)') rather than numbered markers, since author-date is the default mode
Every claim in Findings cites one of the three supplied sources; no orphan or uncited claims
The Discussion section states at least one genuine limitation, not merely a restatement of the Findings

evals.prompt: Review this academic-report draft: it has an Abstract, Background, Method, Findings, and Discussion section, all stating claims about dense retrieval and RAG evaluation as settled fact, and a Discussion that declares the topic 'a solved problem.' There is no References section and no citation of any kind anywhere in the document. What's wrong and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the total absence of citations and a References section as the core, load-bearing defect — the genre's central requirement that every claim trace to a real, verifiable source — and separately flags the unhedged 'solved problem' claim as a violation of the honesty/limitations rule, with concrete fixes for each.
expectations: Names the complete absence of citations and a References section as the central, most load-bearing flaw, not a minor style note
Explains that every claim in an academic report must trace to a real, verifiable cited source, and that omitting a claim is preferable to fabricating a citation for it
Flags the unhedged 'solved problem' framing as a violation of the rule to state limitations honestly and hedge uncertain claims
Recommends adding a genuine References section with sources for every retained claim

evals.prompt: We're evaluating three candidate vector databases against our latency, cost, and durability requirements, and we want a comparison table that grounds a single recommendation. Should this be an academic report?
evals.expected_output: Recommends the engineering genre instead, explaining that a practitioner evaluation built around a mandatory options-vs-criteria comparison table is engineering's defining trait, whereas an academic report is a scholarly IMRaD write-up centered on cited evidence and method rather than a required trade-off matrix. Also distinguishes from google-design-doc's informal prose-driven alternatives.
expectations: Recommends the engineering genre rather than academic for this request
Explains that engineering's defining trait is a mandatory options-vs-criteria comparison table, which this scenario needs and academic does not require
Notes that academic reports are scholarly IMRaD write-ups grounded in cited evidence and explicit method, not vendor/option comparisons
Distinguishes the scenario from google-design-doc, whose alternatives are discursive prose rather than a required table

evals.prompt: Write a formal academic research report on 'sleep duration and cognitive performance' from three sources — Walker (2023) on working-memory impairment under sleep restriction, Van Dongen (2024) on partial recovery of attention after chronic restriction, and Patanaik (2025) on individual differences in vulnerability to sleep loss — but use IMRaD/Vancouver mode: numbered in-text citations like [1], [2] resolving to a numerically ordered reference list, NOT author-date.
evals.expected_output: A complete academic report with all six IMRaD sections, using numbered Vancouver-style in-text citations ([1], [2], [3]) resolving to a numerically ordered References list, with no author-date parentheticals such as '(Walker, 2023)' anywhere in the document.
expectations: Uses numbered in-text citation markers such as [1], [2], [3] consistently throughout, per the requested Vancouver/IMRaD mode
The References section is numerically ordered to match the in-text markers, not alphabetized by author
Contains no author-date parentheticals (e.g. '(Walker, 2023)') anywhere in the document, since the two citation modes must not be mixed
Still contains all six required IMRaD sections despite the change of citation style
